Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mismatch in behavior with MATLAB when using element-wise multiplication (.*) that mixes double and int64 or uint64 inputs
Description: The generated code can produce a wrong answer if your MATLAB code contains an element-wise multiplication?(.*) ?that mixes?double ?and?int64 ?or?uint64 ?inputs. This issue occurs?when all of the following conditions are true:? One input is?double ?and the other is?either?int64 ?or?uint64.  The?double ?input has absolute value greater than or equal to 2^52.  The? int64 ?or?uint64  input has absolute value less than  2^12.   This issue affects MATLAB Coder, MATLAB Function blocks and MATLAB System blocks in Simulink, and MATLAB functions in Stateflow. See also Bug Report ID 1794275 .

Summary: JAR files belonging to installed add-ons are not added to the Java class path in subsequent logins
Description: If an add-on containing a JAR is enabled when you log out of a MATLAB Online session, the expected behavior is for the JAR file to be added to Java class path when you log back in the next time. However the JAR is not added to the Java class path in the new session.?
Workaround: When you log back into a new MATLAB Online session, run the following command to explicitly enable the add-on in the new session. This registers the JAR file with the Java class path.matlab.addons.enableAddon

Summary: MATLAB crashes and memory leaks occur when entity data size is larger than 2 MB
Description: When entity data size is larger than 2 MB: In R2018b, runninga model with SimEvents blocks may crash MATLAB. In R2018a, running amodel with a MATLAB Discrete-Event System block may cause memory leaks and MATLABcrashes when the memory is full after a long simulation.

Summary: graph and digraph plots in an app do not display node or edge labels
Description: If a graph or digraph  is plotted inside an App Designer application or a UIFigure , itsnode and edge labels are not displayed.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: MISRA-compliant Boolean to integer conversions may cause incorrect code
Description: In rare cases, incorrect code might occur for a Simulink model?or MATLAB code with the CastingMode  parameter or property set to Standards.  The problem occurs in MATLAB expressions or Simulink models that mix unsigned and Boolean data in subtraction. The incorrect code occurs?due to a?MISRA compliant Boolean-to-integer type conversion.? The incorrect code uses unsigned literals in the conversion causing the C promotion rules to performthe larger expression as C 'unsigned int' instead of the desired 'signed int'. Here is an example of the incorrect code:? uint16_T u16_a, u16_b;  bool_T b;u16_a <? (b ? 1U : 0U) - u16_b;?  Assume a 32-bit target with a built-in 16-bit type. The above expression might result in a negative value in a run-time evaluation of the right hand side of the comparison. However, the value will be interpreted aspositive in the comparison due to the promotion of unsigned int  from the conditional expressions.? Note that the variables of type uint16_T  promote to signed int  and literals promote to signed int  in absence of a suffix.  One example of correct code is as follows:? u16_a <? (b ? 1 : 0) - u16_b;  The above code is not MISRA compliant because the operands of the subtraction are signed and unsigned respectively.? The code below is both correct and MISRA compliant: u16_a <? (b ? (uint16_T)1 : (uint16_T)0) - u16_b;
Workaround: Avoid combining these operands in Simulink or MATLAB subtraction operations:1) An unsigned? signal or variable that fits into a built-in type smaller than the target word size.? For example, a Simulink signal of size uint16 type will typically fit in a C short on common targets.?? 2) A Boolean signal or variable or logical expression.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Bidirectional port specifications on child model do not get propagated to model reference block during HDL code generation
Description: If a DUT contains a model reference block that has a propagated bidirectional output port, the generated HDL for the DUT does not have its output port specified as bidirectional. As a result, the HDL code does not synthesize correctly.
Workaround: If it is possible, specify the referenced model as Subsystem in which case the bidirectional property is honored.

Summary: Assigning non-scalar input to global variable in MATLAB function called by Interpreted MATLAB Function block can crash MATLAB
Description: The Interpreted MATLAB Function block can?call a MATLAB function with the input signal as its input argument.?This call?can crash MATLAB if The input signal is not scalar In the MATLAB function, the input argument is directly assigned to a global variable. A MATLAB function that can cause such a crash can look like:function y = fcn(u)? ?% ...  ? ?global var;? ?var = u;? ?% ... end

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Calling a custom code function with coder.eval() in Simulink generates incorrect result in code generation
Description: coder.ceval()  in Simulink may give incorrect result in code generation. This?might occur if the following conditions are true: The custom code function called with coder.ceval()  has function array layout settings set to Row-major via the Default function array layout  configuration parameter or the Specify by function  dialog box. Configuration parameter Import custom code  is on.

Summary: Simulation stop button does not work from .NET GUIs
Description: If the MATLAB process loads a .NET assembly that also defined a GUI, and the GUI was used to control an active simulation, the button on the GUI to stop simulation would fail to take effect immediately. Simulation stops much later, based on how the callback for the button to stop simulation was written.

Summary: Help browser fails to display results when searching the locally installed Chinese documentation
Description: When searching the locally installed Chinese documentation, the Help browser fails to display any results.?
Workaround: To work around this issue, you can search the Chinese documentation on the web at https://ww2.mathworks.cn/help/, or search the locally installed English documentation in the Help browser.?

Summary: Sensitivity Analysis model evaluation errors when trying to vary parameters in base MATLAB workspace
Description: When using the Sensitivity Analysis tool, evaluation of the Simulink model errors when trying to vary parameters in the base MATLAB workspace.? The following error is displayed at the command prompt: Warning: Evaluation of function '@(simin)lModelFcn(fcn,sampSetParameters,simin)' failed  with the following errors. Using empty SimulationOutput object as return value.?  Warning: Invalid "PV" argument for the "setPVec" command.?   A similar error may also occur when using sensitivity analysis in the command line, when using the sdo.evaluate command to evaluate the model.? The error occurs if: the sdo.evaluate command is passed the optional options argument, which is an sdo.EvaluateOptions object, and the EvaluatedModel field of the options is set to the sdo.SimulationTest object  The error is:Warning: Evaluation of function '@(s)sdo.internal.simulationManagerExecuteFcn(s,fcn,p)'  failed with the following errors. Using empty SimulationOutput object as return value.?  Warning: Invalid "PV" argument for the "setPVec" command.  Unable to perform assignment because the size of the left side is 1-by-1 and the size  of the right side is 0-by-0.   Error in sdo.evaluate (line 204)  ? ? ? ? ? ? dData(ct,:) = data;
Workaround: If using Sensitivity Analysis tool, store the parameters in the model workspace or a data dictionary instead of the base MATLAB workspace.? If using sensitivity analysis in the command line, use the same workaround just mentioned for Sensitivity Analysis tool.? Or, make sure the options (the sdo.EvaluateOptions object) has its EvaluatedModel field set to the model name, rather than the sdo.SimulationTest object.

Summary: Missing trace data for Simulink Check Abnormal Operating Modes and Tool Interface requirements
Description: The Simulink Check Tool Operational Requirements artifact (qualkitdo_slchk_tor ) contains requirements for the abnormal operating modes and tool interfaces.? However, the required trace data between the requirements and test cases is missing.

Summary: MATLAB might crash when connecting to an NI-VISA serial resource on macOS.
Description: Connecting to an NI-VISA serial resource on macOS using the visa  interface might result in a MATLAB crash. This happens when working with both the Test & Measurement Tool (tmtool ) and command-line workflows.

Summary: On?non-English platform launching?the Excel Wizard may cause Test Manager to hang
Description: On?non-English platform launching?the Excel Wizard may cause Test Manager to hang.?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect compiled information on the Merge block with inactive input port
Description: Incorrect compiled information on the Merge block when all these conditions are true:    Input port of Merge block is inactive and Merge block is active.  Analyze all choices during update diagram and generate preprocessor conditionals  option is not selected on the Variant block.    The Variant block propagates condition to the Merge block. Summary: Incorrect coverage results for Min/Max blocks with Boolean inputs
Description: In cases where the data type of all inputs to the Min/Max block are set to Boolean, the coverage results for the Min/Max block can be incorrect: An unsatisfied objective outcome is reported to be satisfied. A satisfied objective outcome is reported to be not satisfied.
Workaround: If you are performing coverage analysis on your model, do not use Min/Max blocks with Boolean inputs.If Min/Max blocks are used, ensure that the input data types are not Boolean.?Alternatively, consider equivalent logic operations that could be used instead.? For example, the min of two boolean values is equivalent to the AND operation, while the max is equivalent to OR.? Logical Operator blocks are fully compatible with Simulink Coverage; therefore, you may be able to make use of these blocks in your model instead of Min/Max to perform operations on Boolean inputs.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Ordering of loop and function call using matrix incorrectly switched in generated code
Description: The generated code might be incorrect for a MATLAB function that meets all of the following conditions: The function contains two for -loops that execute the same number of iterations. There is a function call between the for -loops. The same matrix is read in either for -loop and is passed as an input to the function call. The function definition corresponding to the call writes to the input argument that passes the matrix.  In the incorrect generated code, the ordering of the access to the matrix in either of the loops and the function call are interchanged. In addition, the function call writes to the same matrix that the loop accesses. Here is an example: function [y1,y2,y3] = issue_1844671(inData)tempData = computeTemp(inData);y1 = zeros(size(tempData));y3 = zeros(200,1);for k = 1:numel(y1)    y1(k) = k .* tempData(k);endy2 = writeThroughInput(tempData);for k = 1:numel(y1)    y3(k) = y3(k) + y1(k);endendfunction out = computeTemp(in)    coder.inline('never');    out = in + 2.*in;endfunction out = writeThroughInput(in)    coder.inline('never');    in = in + 10;    out = zeros(size(in));    for k = 2:numel(out)        out(k) = in(k-1) + in(k);    endend The generated code for this example combines the two loops adjacent to the call to writeThroughInput  into a single-loop and moves the call to writeThroughInput  before the combined loops. This is incorrect because writeThroughInput  modifies the value of tempData  in the generated code before the loop accesses it.void issue_1844671(const double inData[100], double b_y1[100], double y2[100],               double y3[200]){  double tempData[100];  int k;  double d0;  computeTemp(inData, tempData);  memset(&y3[0], 0, 200U * sizeof(double));  writeThroughInput(tempData, y2);  for (k = 0; k < 100; k++) {    d0 = (1.0 + (double)k) * tempData[k];    b_y1[k] = d0;    y3[k] += d0;  }}? Below is the correct generated code. Note that the ordering of the read of tempData  by the loop and call to writeThroughInput  is correct. The function call occurs after the combined loops.?void issue_1844671(const double inData[100], double b_y1[100], double y2[100],               double y3[200]){  double tempData[100];  int k;  double d;  computeTemp(inData, tempData);  memset(&y3[0], 0, 200U * sizeof(double));  for (k = 0; k < 100; k++) {    d = (1.0 + (double)k) * tempData[k];    b_y1[k] = d;    y3[k] += d;  }  writeThroughInput(tempData, y2);}

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Cast that changes the value of its input might be incorrectly removed if used on a bitwise operator
Description: Incorrect code generation might occur for the following patterns in your MATLAB code: out = cast(bitwiseOperator (in1,in2),'type ') relationalOperator  someExpression ;?  out = cast(cast(bitwiseOperator (in1,in2),'type '),'logical');   This issue occurs when all of the following conditions?are true:? ? ? 1. bitwiseOperator   is either bitand , bitor , or bitxor . ? ? 2.? type   is any type that results in the cast changing the value of its input. For example, if in1  and in2  are int64  and type   is uint32 , then this is a value changing cast. ? ? 3.? relationalOperator   is either ==, ~=,?<, <=, >,  or ?>= .? ? ? 4. someExpression  ?is any MATLAB expression. For example, consider the following MATLAB code: function out = foo(in1,in2)out = cast(bitor(in1,in2),'uint32') ~= 0;end Here?in1  and in2  are of type int64 .?The generated code is: boolean_T xg1844659(long in1, long in2){  return (in1 | in2) != 0L;} The cast to 'uint32'  does not appear in the generated code. The correct code should be:? boolean_T foo(long in1, long in2){  long i0;  i0 = in1 | in2;  if (i0 < 0L) {    i0 = 0L;  } else {    if (i0 > 4294967295L) {      i0 = 4294967295L;    }  }  return (unsigned int)i0 != 0U;}

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Sample time inherited by a referenced model can cause mismatch between simulation and code generation results
Description: Code generation results differ from simulation results, when all of the following conditions are true: Top model includes a referenced model. The referenced model inherits a periodic sample time that is slower than the base rate of the top model. The referenced model includes a block that specifies sample time inf  and drives a root outport that inherits the specified sample time inf . The top model and referenced model are configured such that parameters are tunable (model configuration parameter Default parameter behavior  is set to Tunable) . You generate code for the top model.  Even if model parameter Periodic sample time constraint  is set to Ensure sample time independent , the r eference model can pass the check and the code generation results differ from simulation results.
Workaround: Consider one of these approaches: Set model configuration parameter Default parameter behavior to Inlined in the top and referenced models.In the referenced model,?set Periodic sample time constraint to Specified and explicitly specify the sample time that you want the rmodel? to use.Insert a Unit delay block that drives the referenced model outport that uses the sample time specified as a parameter. ?

Summary: Pipe (TL) block does wrong check on number of initial liquid pressures
Description: In Pipe (TL) blocks with the Number of elements  parameter set to 2 , a check done on the size of the Initial liquid pressures  vector may give wrong results.  The check is meant to ensure that the number of initial liquid pressures agrees with the number of pipe segments. A pipe with two segments should have one initial liquid pressure (for a uniform pressure distribution) or two initial liquid pressures (for a linear pressure gradient between the pipe segments).  Instead, the check requires that there be one initial liquid pressure or two initial liquid temperatures ( instead of pressures). The number of initial liquid temperatures is checked separately and it is out of place in a check on initial liquid pressures. As a result, if the pipe has two initial liquid pressures and one initial liquid temperature, the check reports an error when there should be none. If, on the other hand, the pipe has more than two initial liquid pressures and two initial liquid temperatures, the check fails to report an error when in fact there should be one.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Data type of output of accumneg function is incorrect when a signed fixed-point number is subtracted from an unsigned fixed-point number
Description: The accumneg  function casts the second argument to the type of the first argument prior to the subtraction operation. The subtraction uses floor rounding and wrap overflow handling. The result of the subtraction is cast back to the type of the first argument. The issue is that when the first argument is unsigned and the second is signed, the output type is signed, leading to improper quantization of the output. Consider the following example: >> a = fi(67,0,16,8);>> b = fi(-93,1,16,8);>> accumneg(a,b)ans =   -96          DataTypeMode: Fixed-point: binary point scaling            Signedness: Signed            WordLength: 16        FractionLength: 8 ?The output should be unsigned.
Workaround: The following example shows the syntax to work around this issue:>> a = fi(67,0,16,8);>> b = fi(-93,1,16,8);>>c = a;>> c(:) = a - bc =   160          DataTypeMode: Fixed-point: binary point scaling            Signedness: Unsigned            WordLength: 16        FractionLength: 8

Summary: Unexpected error in Command Window when disabling or uninstalling toolboxes with multiple JAR files

When the add-on is disabled or uninstalled, the JAR file is removed from the Java class path. This is achieved by making a call to the MATLAB javarmpath ?function.? If a toolbox contains multiple JAR files, the call to remove the files from the Java class path fails due to a signature mismatch and MATLAB displays an error in Command Window.?
Workaround: As a workaround for this problem, manually remove the JAR files that were included with the toolbox from Java path using javarmpath?function.Note: JAR files can only be removed from the dynamic Java class path. If the JAR files were added to the static Java class path, they cannot be removed for the remainder of the session. This is a known limitation with the Java class path.??

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Constructing a fi object using a numerictype object with unspecified scaling produces incorrect results for built-in integer inputs
Description: If you construct a fi  object with a built-in integer (e.g., int8, uint8)? as the value, and using a numerictype  object with unspecified scaling, the value overflows.  In the following example, the value is a built-in integer, int16(10) , and the numerictype  object uses unspecified scaling, numerictype(1,8) .  The best-precision scaling of the output should have a 3-bit fraction length, but it incorrectly is 7-bit fraction length. This leads to an overflow.The following example shows the issue: >> a = fi(int16(10),numerictype(1,8))a =   0.9922     DataTypeMode: Fixed-point: binary point scaling       Signedness: Signed       WordLength: 8   FractionLength: 7
Workaround: When autoscaling a built-in integer in the fi constructor, do not use a numerictype object.For example, use the Signedness and WordLength properties of the fi object without using a numerictype object, as shown in the following example:>> a = fi(int16(10),1,8)a =   10     DataTypeMode: Fixed-point: binary point scaling       Signedness: Signed       WordLength: 8   FractionLength: 3

Summary: MATLAB crashes when generating?static C/C++ library?code where the code calls a System object?inside the condition expression
